  "content": "",
  "textEditContent": "---\npermalink: /\ntitle: \"About\"\nauthor_profile: true\nredirect_from: \n  - /about/\n  - /about.html\n---\n\n## **Do! Don't just read!**\n\nBAGUS TRIS ATMAJA is a researcher and lecturer in speech and audio processing. He received the B.E. and M.E. degrees from the Sepuluh Nopember Institute of Technology, in 2009 and 2012, respectively, and the Ph.D. degree in information science with a focus on speech emotion recognition from the Japan Advanced Institute of Science and Technology, in 2021. He was employed as a Docent with the Vibrastic Laboratory, Sepuluh Nopember Institute of Technology since 2014. He was also a Postdoctoral and currently a Researcher with the Artificial Intelligence Research Center, National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology (AIST). His research interests include speech processing, affective computing, multimodal recognition, and multitask learning.\n\n## Research Themes\n\nResearch themes offered in areas of speech processing are available [here](https://bagustris.github.io/researchtheme)\n\n## Tutorials\n\n1. [Python Tutorial](http://bagustris.github.io/python-tutorial)\n2. [Tutorial Shell](http://bagustris.github.io/tutorial-shell)\n3. [NLP Python](http://bagustris.github.io/nlp-python)\n4. [Spreadsheet Tutorial](http://bagustris.github.io/spreadsheet-tutorial)\n5. [Octave Tutorial](http://bagustris.github.io/octave-tutorial)\n6. [Tutorial Latex](https://github.com/bagustris/tutorial-latex)\n7. [Tutorial Git](https://bagustris.github.io/tutorial-git)\n8. [Python for signal processing](https://github.com/bagustris/python-for-signal-processing)\n9. [Linear filter](https://github.com/bagustris/linear-filter)\n10. [Speech Recognition](https://bagustris.github.io/speech-recognition-course)\n\n## Blogs\n\n1. [bagustris@/home: random notes](http://bagustris.blogspot.com), including my research notes.\n2. [bagustris@/tumblr: random quotes](https://bagustris.tumblr.com), where I got some inspirations.\n\n## Theses\n\nMy main research areas!\n\n1. Bagus' undergraduate thesis:  \n   [Pemisahan Banyak Sumber Suara Mesin Dari Microphone Array Dengan Metode Independent Component Analysis Untuk Deteksi Kerusakan](https://www.dropbox.com/s/qvifo67qzju99de/TAbagus.pdf?dl=0)\n\n2. Bagus' master thesis:  \n   [On Source Signal Segregation Based On Binaural Inputs](https://www.dropbox.com/s/5wjsrrhxjw5oby3/bta_tesis_en_v16.pdf?dl=0)\n\n3. Bagus' PhD thesis:  \n   [Dimensional Speech Emotion Recognition by Fusing Acoustic and Linguistic Information](https://dspace.jaist.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/10119/17472/2/paper.pdf)\n\n## Publications\n\n1. [Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=xuiLAewAAAAJ&amp;hl=en)\n2. [Scopus](https://www.scopus.com/authid/detail.uri?authorId=36004642300)\n3. [WoS](https://www.webofscience.com/wos/author/record/AAI-5222-2020)\n\n## Research Theme\n\n1. [Style of repository for undergraduate final project](./repo_TA_style)\n2. [(Offered) research theme, feel free to adopt](https://bagustris.github.io/researchtheme)\n\n## Islam\n\n1. [Arbain Nawawi (Syarah Ibn Daqiq)](https://bagustris.github.io/arbain-nawawi)\n2. [Kisah 25 Nabi](https://bagustris.github.io/kisah-nabi)\n\n## Tools\n\n1. [Nkululeko](https://nkululeko.readthedocs.io)\n2. [Speechain](https://bagustris.github.io/speechain)\n\n## Contact\n\nI am open to research collaboration! Contact me by email at bagustris[at]outlook.com (replace [at] with @).\n",
  "toolCallResult": null
}

### Your site is live at:

  "content": "&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\n# The following is the order of the links in the header of the website.\n#\n# Changing the order here will adjust the order and you can also add additional\n# links. Removing a link prevents it from showing in the header, but does not\n# prevent it from being included in the site.\n\nmain:\n  - title: \"Publications\"\n    url: /publications/\n\n  - title: \"Talks &amp; News\"\n    url: /talks/    \n\n  - title: \"Teaching\"\n    url: /teaching/    \n    \n  - title: \"CV\"\n    url: /cv/\n    \n  - title: \"Research\"\n    url: /research/\n    \n  - title: \"Tools\"\n    url: /tools/\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;markdown\n## Research Themes\n\nResearch themes offered in areas of speech processing are available [here](/research/)\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;markdown\n## Research\n\n1. [Style of repository for undergraduate final project](./repo_TA_style)\n2. [(Offered) research theme, feel free to adopt](/research/)\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n",
  "textEditContent": "---\npermalink: /research/\ntitle: \"Research\"\nauthor_profile: true\n---\n\n## Research Theme\n\nMy research goals are aimed at extracting knowledge from acoustic information (aka acoustic features). Examples of this theme include: speech emotion recognition, abnormal sound detection, and audio classification. Moreover, the research can be extended to vibration signals. My approaches to achieve these goals are defined by: (1) data-driven approach (instead of physical modeling), (2) focus on practical implementation (not necessarily following human mechanisms), and robustness (how stable/consistent the model is given any perturbation instead of correctness). For me, science should be evidence-based, implementable, and consistent. My research is result-oriented instead of process-oriented. This doesn't mean that the process (physical phenomena, modeling, math, and algorithms) is not important. If we understand the process very well, the solution may appear by itself. Still, there must be a reason (rationale) for doing such research. Then, I judge my research mainly based on the results. My research contributes to developing technologies to solve issues in Society 5.0 (What is Society 5.0? [Read here in Indonesian language](http://bagustris.blogspot.com/2022/04/menuju-masyarakat-50-melalui-riset-dan.html)).\n\n&lt;!---![research_concept](images/research_concept.png) ---&gt;\n![research_concept_iot](/images/research_concept_iot.png)\n\n## Offered Research Themes\n\nThe following are research themes that I offer, particularly (but NOT limited to) Engineering Physics students at ITS.\n\nFor undergraduate level, I will try to provide the baseline method, and you will improve the results using your proposed method.\n\n1. Speech emotion recognition using multilayer perceptron with CCC loss, dataset: IEMOCAP\n2. Indonesian speech recognition using Wav2Vec2/Hubert/WavLM/UniSpeech-SAT, etc.\n3. Toward universal acoustic features for multi-corpus speech emotion recognition, 30+ datasets.  \n~~4. Predicting Alzheimer's disease using speech analysis.~~\n5. Development of Calfem-Python\n6. Development of Vibration Toolbox  \n~~7. Abnormal sound detection for predictive maintenance (the method is from you/your idea), dataset: DCASE~~\n8. Indonesian emotional speech synthesis using FastSpeech  \n~~9. COVID-19 diagnosis using COUGH sound with deep learning~~\n10. COVID-19 diagnosis using SPEECH sound with deep learning, dataset: ComParE CSS 2021\n11. Predicting pathological voice disorders with speech processing techniques, dataset: SVD, Voiced, HUPA\n12. Detection of emotion intensity in non-speech sounds (laughter, crying, etc.)\n13. Detecting/predicting stuttering (bahasa: gagap) in speech with machine learning\n14. Predicting the intensities of seven self-reported emotions (Adoration, Amusement, Anxiety, Disgust, Empathic Pain, Fear, Surprise) from user-generated reactions to emotionally evocative videos\n15. Few-shot learning on acoustic data to capture 10 dimensions of emotions reliably perceived in distinct vocal bursts: Awe, Excitement, Amusement, Awkwardness, Fear, Horror, Distress, Triumph, Sadness, and Surprise\n16. Multimodal learning (audio+video+text) to capture 10 dimensions of emotions reliably perceived in distinct vocal bursts: Awe, Excitement, Amusement, Awkwardness, Fear, Horror, Distress, Triumph, Sadness, and Surprise\n17. Inferring self-reported emotions from multimodal expression, using multi-output regression to predict fine-grained self-report annotations of seven 'in-the-wild' emotional experiences\n\n## Other Topics/Themes\n\nRead my [papers](https://scholar.google.co.jp/citations?user=xuiLAewAAAAJ&amp;hl=en). Usually, I write down the remaining tasks for future work in that topic.\n\nFor master's level, you can also propose your research theme. Contact me by email for details.\n\n## Typical Timeline\n\n![Timeline for undergraduate](/images/pengerjaan-ta.png)\n\nThe timeline is ideal for undergraduate (S1) students; it can be adapted for master's (S2) and PhD (S3, three years of research from the beginning) levels.\n\n## Style Guide\n\nFor repository style guidelines for undergraduate final projects, see [this guide](./repo_TA_style).\n\n---\n\n**Contact email**: bagus[at]ep.its.ac.id\n# The following is the order of the links in the header of the website.\n#\n# Changing the order here will adjust the order and you can also add additional\n# links. Removing a link prevents it from showing in the header, but does not\n# prevent it from being included in the site.\n\nmain:\n  - title: \"Publications\"\n    url: /publications/\n\n  - title: \"Talks &amp; News\"\n    url: /talks/    \n\n  - title: \"Teaching\"\n    url: /teaching/    \n    \n  - title: \"CV\"\n    url: /cv/\n    \n  - title: \"Research\"\n    url: /research/\n    \n  - title: \"Tools\"\n    url: /tools/## Research Themes\n\nResearch themes offered in areas of speech processing are available [here](/research/)## Research\n\n1. [Style of repository for undergraduate final project](./repo_TA_style)\n2. [(Offered) research theme, feel free to adopt](/research/)",

_**Agent (model copilot/auto)**_

Let me check the structure of the publications and talks pages to understand how they're generated.

The academicpages template uses **collections** to generate the publications and talks pages. Here's how it works:

## Current Structure:

1. **Publications Page**: `_pages/publications.md`
   - Uses the `archive` layout
   - Displays items from the `` collection
   - Each publication is a markdown file in the `` directory

2. **Talks Page**: `talks.html`
   - Uses the `archive` layout  
   - Currently contains static HTML content (your news items)
   - Should display items from the `` collection
   - Each talk is a markdown file in the `` directory

## How to add content:

### For Publications:
Create individual markdown files in `` directory, for example:

````markdown
---
title: "Paper Title"
collection: publications
permalink: /publication/2024-paper-title
excerpt: 'Brief description of the paper'
date: 2024-01-01
venue: 'Conference/Journal Name'
paperurl: 'http://example.com/paper.pdf'
citation: 'Your Name. (2024). "Paper Title." <i>Journal/Conference</i>.'
---
````

### For Talks:
Create individual markdown files in `` directory, for example:

````markdown
---
title: "Talk Title"
collection: talks
type: "Talk"
permalink: /talks/2024-talk-title
venue: "Conference/Event Name"
date: 2024-01-01
location: "City, Country"
---
````

**Note**: Your current talks page has static HTML content. If you want to use the collection-based system like the template intends, you should create individual markdown files in `` for each talk/news item, and the page will automatically generate the list.

Would you like me to:
1. Convert your news items into individual talk/news markdown files in ``?
2. Keep the static HTML approach but improve the formatting?

````markdown
---
layout: archive
title: "Talks"
permalink: /talks/
author_profile: true
---

## News & Updates

* **01/04/2025**: Start working as an assistant professor at somewhere on earth
* **14/01/2025**: [A paper was accepted in ICASSP workshop 2025](https://bagustris.blogspot.com/2025/01/a-paper-was-accepted-at-2025-icassp.html) (SPADE Workshop). A first paper in ICASSP Workshop.
* **06/01/2025**: [A paper was accepted in ICAIIC 2025](https://bagustris.blogspot.com/2025/01/a-paper-was-accepted-at-icaiic-2025.html). A first paper in ICAIIC conference.
* **02/12/2024**: Presenting two papers at TENCON 2024:
  * Multi-Label Emotion Share Regression From Speech Using Pre-Trained Self-Supervised Learning Models
  * Evaluating Hyperparameter Optimization for Machinery Anomalous Sound Detection
* **28/10/2024**: Presenting a paper in MuSe 2024 as part of ACM-MM 2024 in Melbourne, Australia. The paper is listed in this link: [https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3689062.3689082](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3689062.3689082).
* **16/10/2024**: Presenting two papers at O-COCOSDA 2024 in Hsinchu, Taiwan.
  * Check Your Audio Data: Nkululeko For Bias Detection
  * Uncertainty-Based Ensemble Learning For Speech Classification
* **31/05/2024**: A paper was published online in "Journal of Applied Data Science", Title: [Performance Improvement of Covid-19 Cough Detection Based on Deep Learning with Segmentation Methods](https://bright-journal.org/Journal/index.php/JADS/article/view/205)
* **31/10/2023**: Attending APSIPA 2023 in Taipei, Taiwan. I present two papers on speech emotion recognition. It was the first time I had become chair of a conference.
* **04/06/2023**: Attending ICASSP 2023 at Rhodes Island, Greece. In the poster session, I will present my work entitled "EVALUATING VARIANTS OF WAV2VEC 2.0 ON AFFECTIVE VOCAL BURST TASKS"
* **27/02/2023**: Attending Joint ICTP-IAEA Advanced School/Workshop on Machine Learning in Citizen Science at ICTP, Triete, Italy
* **01/01/2023**: I changed my status at AIST from Postdoctoral researcher to Project researcher
* **20/11/2022**: Our paper entitled "Evaluating SSLs for Speech Emotion Recognition" has been accepted on IEEE Access. This is the fifth journal of this year.
* **07/11/2022**: Presented two papers in APSIPA-ASC 2022 in Chiang Mai, Thailand.
* **17/10/2022**: Presented one paper in [ACII 2022](https://acii-conf.net/2022/). First in-person conference after COVID-19.
* **01/04/2022**: My postdoc position in signal processing group, AIRC, AIST Japan will be extended for one year to 30 March 2022.
* **23/03/2022**: Create this news page

---

## Talks and Presentations

{% if site.talkmap_link == true %}

<p style="text-decoration:underline;"><a href="/talkmap.html">See a map of all the places I've given a talk!</a></p>

{% endif %}

{% for post in site.talks reversed %}
  {% include archive-single-talk.html %}
{% endfor %}
````

- Changed file extension from `.html` to `.md`
- Converted `<h2>` tags to `##` markdown headers
- Converted `<ul>` and `<li>` tags to markdown bullet lists using `*`
- Converted nested lists to use proper markdown indentation
- Converted `<strong>` tags to `**bold**` markdown
- Converted `<a href>` tags to `[text](url)` markdown links
- Kept the Jekyll/Liquid template tags (`{% %}`) as they work in both formats
- Removed the `<hr />` tag and replaced with `---` markdown

The page will look the same but now uses cleaner Markdown syntax!

The objective of this research is to utilize Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) to recognize human speech emotion. Based on the accuracy rate the MFCC, number of neurons and layers are adjusted for optimization. Moreover, a custom-made database is introduced and validated using the network optimized. Modelling emotional behaviours is a challenging task due to the variability in perceiving and describing emotions. We try to perform emotion analysis on the speech by collecting speech and textual features and applying a deep neural network model which can classify the sentiments of the speech. Ideally, we would like to experiment with several deep neural network models which take in different combinations of speech features and text as inputs. We propose a Generalized Discriminant Analysis (GerDA) based on DNNs to learn discriminative features of low dimension optimized with respect to a fast classification from a large set of acoustic features for emotion recognition. On nine frequently used emotional speech corpora, we compare the performance of GerDA features and their subsequent linear classification with previously reported benchmarks obtained using the same set of acoustic features classified by Support Vector Machines (SVMs). Modelling emotional behaviours is a challenging task due to the variability in perceiving and describing emotions. We try to perform emotion analysis on the speech by collecting speech and textual features and applying a deep neural network model which can classify the sentiments of the speech. Ideally, we would like to experiment with several deep neural network models which take in different combinations of speech features and text as inputs. Speech features such as Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCC) help retain emotion related low-level characteristics in speech whereas text helps capture the semantic meaning, both of which help in different aspects of emotion detection.downloadDownload free PDFdocument.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', () =&gt; loginButton.addEventListener('click', () =&gt; { document.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent('open-login-modal', options)); }); });View PDFchevron_rightDeep neural networks for acoustic emotion recognition: Raising the benchmarksAndré Stuhlsatz, Thomas Zielke2011 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP), 2011Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) denote multilayer artificial neural networks with more than one hidden layer and millions of free parameters. We propose a Generalized Discriminant Analysis (GerDA) based on DNNs to learn discriminative features of low dimension optimized with respect to a fast classification from a large set of acoustic features for emotion recognition. On nine frequently used emotional speech corpora, we compare the performance of GerDA features and their subsequent linear classification with previously reported benchmarks obtained using the same set of acoustic features classified by Support Vector Machines (SVMs). Our results impressively show that low-dimensional GerDA features capture hidden information from the acoustic features leading to a significantly raised unweighted average recall and considerably raised weighted average recall.downloadDownload free PDFdocument.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', () =&gt; {\n  function getCookie(name) {\n    const value = &#96;; ${document.cookie}&#96;;\n    const parts = value.split(&#96;; ${name}=&#96;);\n    if (parts.length === 2) return parts.pop().split(';').shift();\n  }\n  const csrfToken = document.querySelector(\"[name='csrf-token']\")?.content ??"

IEEE style uses: `[#] Author(s), "Title," *Journal/Conference*, vol. #, no. #, pp. ##-##, Month Year, doi: URL.` Now let me update the remaining years (2019 and earlier): Done! The publications page has been reformatted to **IEEE style** with:

- **Author format**: Initials first, then surname (e.g., B. T. Atmaja)
- **Title in quotes** for papers, *italics* for journal/conference names
- **Volume, number, pages** included where available
- **DOIs as clickable hyperlinks** using `[10.xxx](https://doi.org/10.xxx)` format
- **Alternative links** added where DOIs weren't available (e.g., APSIPA proceedings PDF, JADS journal, Google Drive)
- **Numbered lists** per year section for proper IEEE citation ordering
- **Proper abbreviations** for conferences and journals (e.g., *Proc. IEEE Int. Conf. Acoust., Speech Signal Process.*)
